coredata.dc:title: Facile strategy by hyaluronic acid functional carbon dot-doxorubicin nanoparticles for CD44 targeted drug delivery and enhanced breast cancer therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Jianping
Li, Man
Tian, Lifeng
Qiu, Yue
Yu, Qianwen
Wang, Xuhui
Guo, Rong
He, Qin
coredata.dc:description: A facile approach was developed to synthesize an innovative hyaluronic acid-modified carbon dot-doxorubicin nanoparticles drug delivery platform. CD44 targeted HA-modified carbon dots (HA-CDs) were synthesized as carrier by one-step hydrothermal treatment within one hour with citric acid and branch-PEI as core carbon source. HA not only functioned as carbon dot component but also as hydrophilic group and targeting ligand of this system. The as-prepared HA-CDs were then loaded with doxorubicin (HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX) via an acid-cleavable bond, which released drug in a pH-responsive manner. In in vitro experiments, HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX displayed good hemocompatibility and serum stability, while exhibited high cytotoxicity on 4T1 cells. The confocal laser scanning microscopy and flow cytometry results demonstrated that DOX-loaded nanoparticles were internalized by 4T1 cells via HA-mediated CD44-targeting effect. The enhanced in vivo tumor accumulation of HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX was testified by live imaging. Compared with free DOX, superior in vivo anti-tumor efficacy of HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX was observed in both heterotopic and orthotopic 4T1 cell tumor models. Furthermore, blood hematology and blood biochemistry analysis demonstrated that HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX did not induce noticeable toxicity, which further confirmed the good biocompatibility of HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX. The formulated HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX provides an alternative strategy for targeted breast cancer therapy.

head.ce:title: Facile strategy by hyaluronic acid functional carbon dot-doxorubicin nanoparticles for CD44 targeted drug delivery and enhanced breast cancer therapy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile approach was developed to synthesize an innovative hyaluronic acid-modified carbon dot-doxorubicin nanoparticles drug delivery platform. CD44 targeted HA-modified carbon dots (HA-CDs) were synthesized as carrier by one-step hydrothermal treatment within one hour with citric acid and branch-PEI as core carbon source. HA not only functioned as carbon dot component but also as hydrophilic group and targeting ligand of this system. The as-prepared HA-CDs were then loaded with doxorubicin (HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX) via an acid-cleavable bond, which released drug in a pH-responsive manner. In in vitro experiments, HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX displayed good hemocompatibility and serum stability, while exhibited high cytotoxicity on 4T1 cells. The confocal laser scanning microscopy and flow cytometry results demonstrated that DOX-loaded nanoparticles were internalized by 4T1 cells via HA-mediated CD44-targeting effect. The enhanced in vivo tumor accumulation of HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX was testified by live imaging. Compared with free DOX, superior in vivo anti-tumor efficacy of HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX was observed in both heterotopic and orthotopic 4T1 cell tumor models. Furthermore, blood hematology and blood biochemistry analysis demonstrated that HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX did not induce noticeable toxicity, which further confirmed the good biocompatibility of HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX. The formulated HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX provides an alternative strategy for targeted breast cancer therapy.

ce:section.ce:para: Chemotherapy plays an important role in the cancer clinical therapy. Nevertheless, there is a wide gap between the conventional small molecule chemotherapy drugs and clinical cure rates owning to the relatively poor therapeutic effects and severe adverse reactions caused by small molecules drugs which cannot effectively accumulate in tumor tissues (Peer et al., 2007; Mann. 2002). The emergence of fascinating nanotechnology bridges this gap in recent years. Nanosized drug delivery systems based on nanomaterial, such as liposomes (Park et al., 2012), dendrites (Zhang and Shi. 2013), polymeric nano-materials and inorganic nanoparticles (Cagel et al., 2017; Kyriazi et al., 2018; Parodi et al., 2013), can effectively transport drugs into tumor tissues to increase utilization of drugs, improving curative effect and reducing adverse effects (Hassan et al., 2017; Veiga et al., 2018).

Doxorubicin (DOX) is a chemotherapy drug with broad spectrum of antitumor activity used in cancer treatment. However, severe dose dependent toxicity limited its application (Garsky et al., 2001; Carvalho et al., 2014). To avoid the shortcoming, one of general methods is to conjugate DOX with nanoparticle to deliver it to tumor sites (Ma and Mumper. 2013; Kanwal et al., 2018). Carbon dots (CDs), a class of emerging nanomaterial with good biocompatibility and easy surface modification (Baker and Baker. 2010; Singh et al., 2017), have attracted increasing attention in recent years for their potential application in drug and gene delivery for cancer treatment (Du et al., 2019; Panwar et al., 2019). However, due to lack of specific tumor binding capacity, the unmodified CDs cannot deliver the drug payload into tumor tissues effectively. For this reason, several studies have focused on the construction of active targeting CD-drug delivery systems to enhance the accumulation of drugs for better treatment effects. A CD-DOX nanocomplex was prepared by Mewada et al. using folic acid as a targeting ligand (Mewada et al., 2014). Yang et al. prepared a nucleus target signal peptide functionalized carbon dots to transport DOX (Yang et al., 2016). Bao and co-workers constructed a mPEG-OAL modified, pH-responsive carbon quantum dots-DOX drug delivery platform, which enhanced tumor targeting efficiency (Bao et al., 2019).

Polymers, such as lignin (Chen et al., 2016), chitosan and polyethylene glycol (Janus et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2015), are excellent candidates for the synthesis of carbon dots. Hyaluronic acid (HA), a naturally polysaccharide, is an attractive target ligand for binding with CD44 receptors overexpressed on various tumor cells (Cohen et al., 2014). Besides, the long chain structure and negative charges of HA may endow it with “stealth effect” and hydrophilicity which is similar to polyethylene glycol (He et al., 2013). Owning to those characteristics and along with the excellent properties in biocompatibility, non-toxicity, biodegradability, and non-immunogenicity, HA is considered as an optimal candidate for preparing carbon dots for drug delivery (Mizrahy and Peer. 2012; Cai et al., 2019).

However, most of the current CD-drug delivery and many other small nanoparticle drug delivery systems are formulated by step-by-step modification methods (Zhu et al., 2011; Guo et al., 2019; Cheng et al., 2015). Therefore, there is special theoretical and practical significance to prepare active target drug delivery system in a facile strategy. To address this problem, an interesting approach was proposed in the present manuscript to prepare carbon dots in a one-step method, which would not only be suitable for conjugating anti-tumor drugs, but also enhance active targeting capacity. Encouraged by the all the above-mentioned reasons, in this work, a CD44-targeted carbon dot-drug delivery system was constructed with HA by a one-step method. HA modified carbon dot drug delivery system (HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX) was constructed by conjugating hydrophobic doxorubicin with HA-CD through 4-Carboxybenzaladehyde (p-CBA) as pH-sensitive linker (concrete synthetic procedure in Scheme 1 ) To our best knowledge, this construction strategy has not been reported yet. In this study, various techniques were used to characterize the as-prepared complexes. The in vitro cytotoxicity and hemocompatibility were evaluated. The competitive antagonistic experiment was conducted to evaluate the targeting properties in vitro through confocal fluorescence microscopy and flow cytometry. The selective accumulation at the tumor tissues of the HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX was evaluated by the NIR imaging experiment in vivo. The in vivo therapeutic efficacy of HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX was evaluated in heterotopic and in situ 4T1 cell tumor models on BALB/c mice. For the systematic toxicity assay, the hematology analysis and blood biochemistry analysis were performed.

Citric acid (Mw = 192.14) and Branched polyethylenimine (Mw = 10,000) were purchased from Shanghai Chemical Reagents Corporation (SCRC). Hyaluronic acid (Mw = 3000–10,000) and 4-Carboxybenzaladehyde (p-CBA) were purchased from Freda Biopharm Co., and Shanghai Aladdin Chemical Co., respectively. (1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride) (EDC) was purchased from J & K Scientific Ltd. and N-hydroxyl succinimide (NHS) was purchased from Energy Chemical Shanghai Co., Ltd. Adriamycin hydrochloride was obtained from Dalian Meilun Biotechnology Co., Ltd. All chemicals were used as received without further purification.

Plastic cell culture dishes and plates were obtained from Wuxi NEST Biotechnology Co. (Wuxi, China). BALB/c mice purchased from Chengdu Dossy Experimental Animals Co., Ltd. All animal experiments were performed in accordance with the principles of care and use of laboratory animals and were approved by the experiment animal administrative committee of Sichuan University.

The morphology of the HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX was observed by transmission electron microscope (TEM) operated at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Surface charges (zeta potentials) and sizes were tested at 25 °C by Nano-ZS Zetasizer dynamic light scattering detector (Malvern Instruments, UK). UV/vis spectra were recorded with a Shimadzu UV-240 spectrophotometer. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured using Magna IR-750 (Nicolet Instrument Co. USA). The NMR hydrogen spectroscopy measurements were conducted on Varian UNITY INOVA400 (400 MHz, USA) and ACE200 (200 MHz, Germany).

The HA-modified carbon dots (HA-CDs) were synthesized through a short-time hydrothermal method. In a typical experiment, 200 mg citric acid, 120 mg HA, were dissolved in deionized-water respectively. Then, these obtained samples were added into 400 mg branched-poly (ethylene mine) (PEI) aqueous solution by intense magnetic stirring to form 12 mL uniform mixture. The mixture was transferred into a 25 mL stainless-steel autoclave vessel and was heated in a furnace at 180 °C for 60 min. After cooling down to room temperature, the resulting brown aqueous products were purified by vacuum filtration and ultrafiltration (MWCO = 50,000) at 4000 rpm × 20 min for 4 times to obtain the HA-CDs.

For the synthesis of HA-CD@p-CBA, the typical preparation method and procedure as follows: 4-Carboxybenzaladehyde (p-CBA) 20 mg, (1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride) (EDC) 25.47 mg and N-hydroxyl succinimide (NHS) 15.33 mg were dispersed in 2 mL anhydrous dichloromethane, this mixture were reacted for 4 h at room temperature until the system was homogeneous transparent solution. After removal of dichloromethane by vacuum rotary evaporation, 10 mL DMSO was added to re-dissolve it, 40 mg HA-CDs suspend in 1 mL water at pH 7.4 was slowly added into the DMSO dispersion system drop by drop. After reaction for 6 h, the reaction system dialyzed three times (MWCO = 3500) and then concentrated to about 5 mL by ultrafiltration tube (MWCO = 30,000) to obtain the product HA-CD@p-CBA. Finally, the HA-CD@p-CBA dispersion were dialyzed against DMSO (MWCO = 3500) for the following reaction.

The conjugation schematic diagram of hydrophobic doxorubicin and HA-CD as shown in Scheme 1. Firstly, doxorubicin hydrochloride 20 mg and 11.5 μL triethylamine dissolve in 2 mL anhydrous DMSO to allow reaction for 12 h to get hydrophobic doxorubicin (doxorubicin dehydrochloride). Then, the obtained-hydrophobic doxorubicin was added to HA-CD@p-CBA, after 24 h reaction, 43 mL deionized water was added and ultra-filtrated (MWCO = 30,000, 2000 rpm × 20 min) for several times until the ultrafiltration effluent is colourless. The ultraviolet spectrophotometry was used to quantify the doxorubicin of ultrafiltration effluent to get the doxorubicin loading of retentate. The product was freeze-dried for the further characterization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro drug release behaviour of HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX
ce:section.ce:para: Dialysis method was used to test the in vitro doxorubicin release. An aliquot of each HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX (doxorubicin 100 μg mL−1, 3 mL) or free doxorubicin was placed into dialysis tube (MWCO = 3500). After tightly packed, the dialysis tubes were immersed into 50 mL PBS (pH 5.5, pH 6.8 and pH 7.4) and were incubated in an incubator under 37 °C/75 rpm for 24 h. At each predetermined time point, 3 mL release medium was sampled and equal volume of fresh release medium was added. Then, the samples were determined by ultraviolet spectrophotometer at wavelength 484.0 nm. According to the standard curve, the doxorubicin content and release rate were calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro stability of HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX in serum
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX in serum was investigated by measuring the mixture turbidity changes of HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX in 50% fatal bovine serum (FBS) system. Briefly, different concentrations of HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX (40 μg.mL−1, 20 μg mL−1 and 10 μg mL−1) in HEPES buffer solution were mixed with equal volume FBS. Then, the mixtures were incubated at 37 °C with 75 rpm shaking and sampled at each predetermined time point (0, 1, 2, 4, 8, 12 and 24 h). A microplate reader (Thermo Scientific Varioskan Flash, USA) was used to measure the transmittance of each sample at 750 nm. HEPES buffer containing 50% FBS and HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX mixed with equal volume of HEPES buffer were considered as blank control and negative control, respectively.

As our group described previously, the hemocompatibility assay was performed to evaluate the safety of HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX during body circulation (Shi et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2013) .Briefly, fresh mouse whole blood was collected in heparin sodium-containing tubes. Red blood cells (RBC) were separated from whole blood by centrifugation at 3000 rpm for 5 min, and then diluted with normal saline to a concentration of 2% (v/v). The 2% RBC (v/v) were incubated with the equal volume of different concentrations of HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX (40 μg mL−1, 20 μg mL−1, 10 μg mL−1, 5 μg mL−1 and 1 μg mL−1) at 37 °C with shaking at 75 rpm. At each predetermined time point, samples were centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 5 min and followed by the absorbance of haemoglobin in supernatant were measured using a microplate reader (Thermo Scientific Varioskan Flash) at 540 nm. RBC (2%) treated with 1% Triton X-100 or normal saline was set as positive or negative control, respectively.

4T1 cells (marine breast carcinoma cells) were cultured in RPMI-1640 medium supplemented with 10% FBS, 100 U/mL streptomycin, and 100 U/mL penicillin at 37 °C in a humidified 5% CO2 atmosphere. The medium was replaced every 1–2 days.

For cell imaging assay of CLSM, 4T1 cells were seeded on 18 mm glass coverslip in a six-well plate at a density of 0.8 × 105 cells per well. After 24 h cells attachment incubation, HA diluted in serum-free culture media (2.5 mg mL−1) was added into each well of HA inhibit group for 2 h further incubation. Then HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX was added into the competitive inhibiting group and free HA group at a final DOX concentration of 10 μg mL−1. After incubation for another 4 h, the cells were rinsed several times with PBS (pH 7.4), fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde at room temperature for 15 min and then stained the nuclei with DAPI for 10 min. Finally, fluorescence images were acquired under excitation wavelength at 359 nm, 401 nm and 494 nm of a laser scanning confocal microscope. All observations were performed under the same conditions.

4T1 cells were seeded onto six-well plate at a density of 1 × 106 cells per well and incubation for 24 h under 37 °C to allow cells attachment. After the HA inhibit group incubation with HA (2.5 mg mL−1) for another 2 h, HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX were added into the competitive inhibiting group and free HA group at a DOX dose of 5 μg mL−1. After incubation under 37 °C for 4 h, the cells were washed with cold PBS for three times, trypsinized and suspended in 0.4 mL PBS. The fluorescent intensity of cells was measured by flow cytometer (CytomicsTM FC 500, Beckman Coulter, Miami, FL, USA) with the excitation wavelength at 488 nm and the emission wavelength at 525 nm. Ten thousand events were recorded for each sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of dynamic endocytosis and intracellular location
ce:section.ce:para: 4T1 cells were seeded onto six-well plate at a density of 1 × 106 cells per well and incubation for 24 h under 37 °C to allow cells attachment. The free doxorubicin and HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX were added in to each well at a final doxorubicin concentration of 5 μg mL−1. At predetermined time points of 1 h, 2 h and 4 h, the cells were rinsed several times with PBS (pH 7.4), fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde at room temperature for 15 min and then stained the nuclei with DAPI for 10 min. After rinsed with PBS and mounted by resin, fluorescence images were acquired by confocal microscopy system under each excitation wavelength. The subcellular distribution of HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX was detected by using confocal laser scanning microscopy. Cells were seeded on coverslip attachment in six-well plate as previous. After incubation with doxorubicin or HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX (at a DOX dose 5 μg mL−1) for 4 h, cells were washed with cold PBS for 3 times to remove the drug-containing medium. Lyso-tracker DND-22 was dissolved in serum-free medium to a concentration 100 nM and was then added into each well followed by standard procedure. The cells were fixed by 4% paraformaldehyde at room temperature for 15 min and then DAPI was applied to stain the nuclei for another 10 min. Cold PBS rinsed the cells three times and coverslips were taken out and mounted by resin for confocal lasers scanning.

The cytotoxicity of HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX and free doxorubicin against 4T1 cells were evaluated by MTT (3-(4, 5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2, 5-diphenyl-2-H-tetrazolium bromide) assay method. 4T1 cells were seeded into 96-well plates at a density of 2 × 103 cells per well and incubation at 37 °C for 24 h. Then, the cell media were replaced by serum-free medium containing different final doxorubicin concentrations with pH adjusted to either pH 7.4 or pH 6.3. After another 24 h incubation, the doxorubicin containing media were replaced by 100 μL MTT solution (1000 μg mL−1 in medium) and further incubation for 4 h at 37 °C. Next, the MTT solution was discarded and 100 μL dimethyl sulfoxide was added into each well. Finally, the absorbance was measured by a microplate reader (Thermo Scientific Varioskan Flash) at 490 nm after incubation for 10 min at 37 °C with gentle shaking. Cell viability (%) was calculated as Atest-Ablank/Acontrol-Ablank × 100%, where Atest and Aconrol represented the absorbance of cells treated with different test solutions and blank culture media respectively. Each test concentration group was performed with five parallel wells under identical conditions.

BALB/c female mice (6–8 week-old, 18–22 g) were employed in this study. Tumour-bearing mice model was established as following: each mouse were inoculated 0.1 mL 4T1 cell suspension subcutaneously into right flank (heterotopic tumour model) or right second mammary fatty pad (orthotopical tumour model) at the density of 1 × 107/ml in PBS. After tumours reached about 50–100 mm3, the 4T1 tumour-bearing mice were randomly divided into PBS, doxorubicin hydrochloride and HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX group.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo fluorescence imaging and tissue distribution
ce:section.ce:para: The tumour bearing mice were randomly divided into three groups for in vivo experiment. Indocyanine green (ICG) and indocyanine green labelled HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX (HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX@ICG) were injected intravenously at a dose 2.5 mg ICG/kg. At 1 h, 4 h, 8 h, and 24 h after injection, the mice were anesthetized and subsequently imaged by the IVIS® Spectrum (Perkin Elmer). At each predetermined time point, mice were sacrificed after heart perfusion with PBS and the heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney and tumour were removed for imaging.

Treatment of 4T1 tumour-bearing BALB/c mice (heterotopic tumour model or orthotopical tumour model) started on the 6th day when the tumours reached about 50–100 mm3. 4T1 tumour-bearing mice were randomly assigned into three groups: PBS, doxorubicin hydrochloride and HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX group. On the 6th, 8th, 10th, 12th and 14th day, each group was intravenously given the doxorubicin preparations or PBS through tail vein at DOX dose of 3 mg kg−1. The mice weight and tumour volume were measured and recorded every other day from the first day of administration. On the 21th day after tumour inoculation, mice were sacrificed to obtain main tissues and tumours. The tumours were weighted and the organs and tumours were collected for sections and subjected to haematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX effect on blood system in mice
ce:section.ce:para: The systematic safety of HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX was evaluated by blood biochemistry assay and blood routine examination. After 24 h of completion of therapy, blood samples were collected from orbital vain to test the complete blood routine by animal blood cell analyser, and the plasma samples were collected following centrifuged blood samples at 5000 rpm for 10 min to detect serum alanine aminotransferase (ALT), aspartate aminotransferase (AST), creatinine (CRE), blood urea nitrogen (BUN) and creatine kinase (CK) levels.

Experiments were performed at least in triplicate unless otherwise noted. Statistical comparisons were performed by one-way ANOVA for multiple groups. Significant differences between or among groups were indicated by *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01 ***p < 0.001 and ****p < 0.0001, respectively.

As carbon source, citric acid was usually used in the synthesis of carbon nanoparticles through pyrolysis. Citric acid and amino or hydroxyl group-containing molecules, such as PEG (Yang et al., 2015), glycine (Yan et al., 2015), PEI (Liu et al., 2012), and ethylenediamine (Jing et al., 2019), are believed to be suitable precursors to synthesize carbon nanoparticles because of carboxyl groups of citric acid firstly polymerized with amine or hydroxyl groups to form amide or ester bond. This mechanism may also be applicable for simplifying carbon quantum dots synthesis by using citric acid, hyaluronic acid and PEI as starting materials. These HA-CDs were synthesized in relative mild conditions within 1 h, with PEI and HA residuals remaining on the surface of carbon dots. After an intermediate HA-CD@p-CBA was synthesized by coupling p-CBA and HA-CD through acid-sensitive imine bond, the anticancer drug DOX was conjugated with HA-CDs to form HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX. The optimal preparation method and prescription of HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX was analysed and shown in Tables S1–S5†.

In this work, the size and morphology were characterized by DLS and TEM techniques. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images in Fig. 1 A showed that the HA-CD particles were spherical and well dispersity with a diameter about 10 nm, basically consistent with the hydrated state particle size measured by DLS (~13.41 nm, Fig. 1A). The size diameter in our work is slightly larger than that conventional carbon dots, which may be attributed to the HA corona attached on the surface of carbon dots. As shown in Fig. 1B, the HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX nanocomplexes displayed a well-dispersed sub-spherical morphology with an average statistical diameter of about 30 nm which was consistent with the result of DLS measurement (38.61 ± 14.30 nm, PDI: 0.214). This result may be attributed to the conjugation doxorubicin and the increased hydrophobicity is probably caused by dehydrochloride of doxorubicin. The surface charges (zeta potentials) of HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX was detected that is +15.200 ± 0.889 mV (n = 3), which is an important parameter for the long-term stability.

The obtained product was further characterized by the UV/vis and fluorescence spectroscopies. Similar to other reports, the fluorescence spectrum of HA-CD displayed a bright blue color emission upon UV light irradiation, where an emission peak centered at 461 nm was observed in the emission spectrum under the radiation at maximum UV excitation wavelength of 341 nm (Fig. 1C). The UV/vis spectra of as-prepared HA-CD and HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX were shown in Fig. 1D. Two characteristic bands were observed at 240 and 350 nm in the typical UV/vis absorption spectrum of HA-CD, which could be assigned to aromatic CC π-π* transition (~240 nm) and CO n-π* transition (~340 nm) (Li et al., 2014; Mohapatra et al., 2015), respectively. The successful conjugation of doxorubicin with HA-CD was certified by UV/vis spectrum. Two strong peaks at 350 nm and 500 nm belonged to the characteristic peaks of HA-CD and doxorubicin, respectively.

FT-IR spectral measurement was performed to identify the corresponding functional groups of HA-CD and HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX (Fig. 2 A, B and Fig. S1†). As shown in Fig. 2A, the broad peaks from 3100 to 3500 cm−1 were attributed to the νNH and νOH related to primary amide, secondary amide, and amino group stretching vibrations. The distinctive peaks at 2953, 2936, 2883, 2836, 1414 and 1376 cm−1 represented νas C
H, νs C
H, δas C
H and δs C
H stretching and bending vibrations, which belonged mainly to the CH2 of PEI and CH3 of HA, separately. The peaks centered at 1048 cm−1 were attributed mostly to νCO of HA carboxyl group. 1H NMR was carried out to further certify the chemical structure (Fig. 2C). In the 1H NMR spectra, the chemical shift from 2.4 to 2.8 ppm belonged to protons on the CH2N of PEI and 1.93 ppm referred to the characteristic NMR peaks of HA. According to the FT-IR and 1H NMR spectra analysis, a conclusion can be drawn that HA and PEI residual remain on the surface of as-prepared HA-CDs, even after the hydrothermal synthesis procedure. The chemical structure of HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX complexes were also characterized by FT-IR and 1H NMR methods (Fig. 2B and D). The peak located at 1634 and 1678 cm−1 of HA-CD@p-CBA represented the amide and aldehyde stretching vibration of νCO, which attribute to activated carboxyl group of p-CBA firstly reacted with amino of HA-CD to form amide structure and retained aldehyde group of HA-CD@p-CBA. The two peaks of HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX at 1623 and 1554 represented the stretching vibration peak of νCN and/or the amide correlation peak of HA-CD@ p-CBA. The typical vibration frequency of νCN located at different wave number from ~1580 to 1690 cm−1 according to concrete chemical environment(Jin et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2019; Tao et al., 2018). HA-CD@B-DBA, the imidization product of Benzene-1, 4-dicarboxaldehyde (B-DBA) and HA@CD was used to further testify the reaction between HA-CD and p-CBA (Fig. S1†). In Fig. S1†, the FT-IR peaks of HA-CD@B-DBA located at 1632 and 1552 cm−1 represented amide bond of HA-CD and νCN of HA-CD@B-DBA. Most importantly, the curve showed two new peaks at 1014 and 951 cm−1 corresponding to the imine group. To compare three curves, HA-CD@p-CBA without those kind of peaks, while the same absorption peaks appeared at HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX spectrum. This result confirmed that the activated carboxyl group of p-CBA firstly reacted with amine of HA-CD to form aldehyde modified intermediate HA-CD@p-CBA, then, the aldehyde group conjugated with primary amino group of hydrophobic DOX to synthesize the final product HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX. The typical chemical shift of benzene ring appearing at 7.79 and 7.58 ppm indicated the successful conjugation between p-CBA and HA-CDs. Two distinct peaks were observed at 1.09 and 3.50 ppm in the spectrum of HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX, which were assigned to CH3 of pyranose ring and OCH3 of benzene of DOX, separately. The analysis results based on FT-IR and 1H NMR spectra certified that the HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX was successful synthesized with p-CBA as a linker.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DOX loading efficiency and in vitro DOX release study
ce:section.ce:para: UV/vis spectroscopy was adopted to quantify the DOX loading amount on the HA-CDs by measuring the absorbance at 484.0 nm. According to the measured amount of DOX in the effluent, the DOX loading amount and drug loading efficiency were calculated to be 2215 μg (per 10 mg HA-CD) and 18.13%, respectively. The drug release behaviour was investigated at pH 7.4, 6.8 and 5.5 to certify the acid-responsive drug release property. As shown in Fig. 3 A, less than 20% DOX was released under normal pH 7.4 condition while over 50% DOX was released with an accelerated release performance at pH 5.5 within 24 h. This results indicated that DOX could be released from HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX in the acidic intracellular compartments due to the acid cleavable character of Schiff base.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro hemocompatibility assays and in vitro stability in serum
ce:section.ce:para: In vitro hemolysis assay was conducted to evaluate the hemocompatability of HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX. The hemolysis profiles were expressed as the percentage of haemoglobin release relative to the positive and negative controls, and the hemolysis less than 10% was always regards as biocompatible (Fischer et al., 2003). Erythrocytes were incubated with HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX ranging from 0.5 to 20 μg mL−1 for 4 h. No significant hemolysis or any concentration-dependent increase in hemolysis was observed, illustrating that HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX were biocompatible (Fig. 3B and Fig. S2†). Fischer et al. demonstrated that 1 mg mL−1 PEI (600–1000 kD) caused complete lysis of the erythrocytes and Jain et al. reported that PEI caused hemolysis could be significantly reduced by PEI modified with hyaluronic acid (Fischer, et al., 2003; Jain et al., 2015). As a result, the well-hemocompatibility of HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX could be explained by the surface modification with HA which avoided the direct interact between the cationic core and the negative membrane of erythrocytes. The stability of HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX was tested in 50% FBS. As illustrated in Fig. 3C, three different concentration preparations were well-dispersed that the transmittance showed little change in HEPES buffer containing 50% FBS over 24 h. This also should be attributed to the surface modification with HA which prevented the direct interaction between cationic core of HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX and serum.

The optical spectra have testified that some HA residues remained on the surface of the HA-CDs and HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX. Natural HA macromolecule can specific target to CD 44, which is responsible for the recognition, binding and internalization of HA (Mattheolabakis et al., 2015; Culty et al., 1992). For this reason, HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX may have the capacity to target 4T1 cells overexpressing CD 44 receptor. In order to verify this hypothesis, a HA competitive inhibition test was performed to evaluate the CD 44 receptor-mediated endocytosis capacity of HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX in 4T1 cells. As shown in Fig. 4 A, a higher positive rate was observed from CD@DOX group than that of HA pre-incubation group, indicating that 4T1 cells could effectively internalize a large number of HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX, but the endocytosis was markedly inhibited by preincubation with free HA. A quantitative analysis was conducted to compare the positive rate of the two groups (Fig. 4B). The results showed that after pre-treatment with free HA, the cellular uptake decreased by 37.43% compared to that of HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX group. A decreased fluorescence intensity was also observed in confocal laser scanning microscope images in free HA pre-incubation group (Fig. 4C). This result was well consistent with that of flow cytometry. Because free HA competitively pre-occupied the CD 44 receptors, HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX might not be effectively recognized and internalized by CD 44 receptor high-expressing cells. Based on this, such a decrease was reasonable and it also indicated that HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX had the capacity target CD44 expressing cells. According to the above-mentioned, the CD44 receptors mediated endocytosis was responsible for the efficient intracellular delivery of HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of dynamic endocytosis and intracellular location of HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX
ce:section.ce:para: These studies were performed by using CLSM. A dynamic endocytosis process was observed in the nanocomplexes group comparing with free DOX group (Fig. 5 A). A dynamic increase process was observed in HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX group that cell uptake gradually increased from 1 h to 4 h and peaked at 4 h, which was very different from doxorubicin reaching its maximum at the first hour. The intracellular translocation process was studied. As shown in Fig. 5B, HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX mainly distributed in lysosome at 4 h. In view of this result, it might be explained by the fact that HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX could be readily internalized into the lysosomes via HA receptor-mediated endocytosis. Doxorubicin could be released from HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX by cleavage of the hydrazone bonds in the endo/lysosome, facilitating its accumulation in the nucleus to further exert the antiproliferative activity (Yue et al., 2008).

MTT assay was performed to evaluate the cytotoxicity of 4T1 cells under different conditions (Fig. S3†). HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX or free DOX played important roles in 4T1 cells viability in a concentration-dependent manner. Cell viability was generally maintained at over 90% after incubation with HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX at pH 7.4 for 24 h (DOX: 0.01 μg mL−1). An obvious cytotoxicity was observed with the cell viability reduced to 40% when cells were exposed to 10 μg mL−1 doxorubicin. The results showed a higher cytotoxicity in free doxorubicin groups than HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX under the same condition. This was probably because free doxorubicin could easily cross the cell membrane via free diffusion while the release of doxorubicin from HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX depended on the cleavage of acid-sensitive chemical bond.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo fluorescence imaging and tumor delivery of HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX
ce:section.ce:para: An in vivo distribution assay was performed to evaluate the tumor accumulation performance of HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX by using ICG as a nearinfrared (NIR) probe. The orthotopical 4T1 tumor-bearing BALB/c mice were intravenously injected with ICG loaded HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX (Characterization was shown in Fig. S4†) or free ICG at the dose of 2.5 mg kg−1 via the tail vein. As shown in Fig. 6 A, the fluorescence intensity of ICG loaded HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX at tumor sites gradually increased and reached the maximum at 4 h, which further lasted up to 24 h post injection. In comparison, only low fluorescence intensity could be observed at 1 h in free ICG treated mice. This efficient tumor accumulation should be ascribed to the HA residues on the nanoparticles surface which increased the tumor accumulation through CD 44 mediated targeting and EPR effect of HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX (Nakamura et al., 2016). Dissected major organs and tumors were harvested and examined by fluorescence imaging. Similar results were observed that excised tumors of HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX group showed stronger fluorescence intensity compared with those treated with free ICG (Fig. 6B and C). In HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX group, strong fluorescence were also observed in livers while relatively weak fluorescence appeared in lungs and kidneys, probably owing to the endocytic hyaluronan receptor of liver sinusoidal endothelial cells responsible for the clearance of HA and other glycosaminoglycans from the circulation (Fig. S5†) (Zhou et al., 1999). The enhanced tumour accumulation of HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX should be attributed to the HA modification which not only endowed the nanoparticles with a capacity of active target to tumours by HA mediation but also gave it the similar “stealth effect” observed in PEGylated nanoparticles, which extended the systemic circulation (He et al., 2013; Yoon et al., 2013).

The antitumor efficiency of HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX was evaluated in heterotopic and orthotopic 4T1 cell tumor models on BALB/c mice. The antitumor effects were shown in Figs. 7 and 8 . The time-related tumor growth was observed. Both free DOX and HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX could inhibit tumor growth to different extents while rapid growth was exhibited in PBS group. According to the tumor weight of heterotopic tumor models, an outstanding antitumor effect was observed in HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX treated group with the relative tumor growth inhibitory rate of 78.53%. In comparison, free DOX only represented moderate antitumor efficacy with the relative tumor growth inhibitory rate of 57.07%. This result is in accordance with the orthotopic tumor model treatment. The relative tumor growth inhibitory rates were 58.28% and 80.23% in free DOX and HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX group, respectively. The differences in treatment results were statistically significant when HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX group was compared to either the free DOX group or PBS control (p < 0.001 versus PBS and p < 0.05 or 0.001 versus free DOX). The above-mentioned results of in vivo antitumor studies demonstrated that the HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX was effective in inhibiting tumor growth in 4T1 heterotopic and orthotopic tumor models, which should be attributed to the enhanced tumor accumulation mediated by HA and EPR effect. Body weight changes were measured every 2 days during the treatment to synchronous monitor the toxicity. Compared with the initial body weight before treatment, no obvious body weight shift was observed in mice treated with HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX, while the free DOX treated group showed about 15% weight loss, indicating that the HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX could effectively reduce the systemic toxicity of DOX. Histopathology analysis was conducted to further investigate any potential toxicity of HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX. H&E-stained sections of the main organs, including heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney, were presented in Figs. 7E and 8E. No histopathological abnormality, degeneration or pathological changes were observed in the sections of HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX treated group. In comparison, myocardial fiber rupture indicated that free DOX treatment caused severe cardiotoxicity, which was consistent with the result of blood biochemistry analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX effect on blood system
ce:section.ce:para: The hematology analysis was conducted to further investigate any potential in vivo toxicity of HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX. Blood routine test and blood biochemical index analysis were performed after 24 h of treatment. In peripheral blood routine count (Table S6†), the white blood cell and lymph cell levels in free DOX and HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX group were lower than that in control group. For other markers, no obvious differences were observed in red blood cells, platelet (PLT) and hemoglobin levels among the three groups. In terms of blood biochemical indexes test (Fig. 9 ), there were no differences in AST, ALT, BUN and Cre levels among the 3 groups, indicating that HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX did not show obvious adverse effects on liver and kidney functions. Cardiotoxicity is an important index for the application of containing DOX preparations. In terms of cardiac functions, the results demonstrated that there were no difference in CK levels between HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX and PBS control group, while DOX group showed significant increase in CK levels (Fig. 9D), suggesting that HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX did not induce cardiotoxicity at indicated dosage. The hemocompatibility and histocompatibility as well as enhanced antitumor effect of HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX could be attributed to the HA modification and targeted sustained DOX release behaviour, suggesting that the HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX nano-complexes may be worthy of further research for the antitumor therapy.

This work presented a CD 44 targeted HA-modified carbon dot-doxorubicin nanoparticles drug delivery platform (HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX). HA-modified carbon dots (HA-CDs) were prepared by one-pot hydrothermal method within one-hour using citric acid, branch-PEI and hyaluronic acid as precursors. The HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX was constructed through an acid-cleavable bond conjugation between DOX and HA-CD, which was further confirmed by TEM, UV/vis, FT-IR and 1H NMR. In addition, the drug-loading rate of HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX was 18.12% and the drug release in vitro was acid responsive. The presence of HA endowed the HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX with good stability and hemocompatibility. More importantly, the HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX showed high cytotoxicity to 4T1 cells after cellular internalization via CD 44 targeted effect. Furthermore, the enhanced accumulation of the HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX into tumor tissue was confirmed by the NIR imaging experiment in vivo, which led to enhanced anti-tumor activity compared with free DOX. HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX was proven a safe and effective anti-tumor drug delivery system.
